title: 1980s America
description: Test your knowledge of the 1980s in the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
What are the principal policies of **supply-side economics**?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Supply-side economics did not involve increasing taxes.

choices.content: Cut taxes; deregulate industry
Increase taxes to eliminate the federal deficit; regulate multi-national corporations
Regulate the federal minimum wage; invest in clean energy

assessment_items.question_content:
What was the Reagan administration hoping to achieve by **deregulating** some American industries?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: With less government regulation, employers would be more likely to decrease wages than increase them.

choices.content: It hoped to lower costs for employers, leading to cheaper consumer goods.
A greater supply of oil from American wells, which were hampered from reaching their full potential due to environmental regulations
Higher wages for employees as minimum wages were set too low by state governments

assessment_items.question_content:
Why was **Ronald Reagan** known as “The Great Communicator”?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Reagan's nickname was not related to telecommunications.

choices.content: Trained as an actor, Reagan was a skilled and comfortable public speaker.
Reagan was particularly adept at building consensus among opposing factions in Congress.
Reagan’s administration sponsored university research into improving telecommunications.

assessment_items.question_content:
How did Ronald Reagan’s 1984 reelection ad, **Morning in America**, capture his attitude about the nation’s future?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Soviet Union did not fall during Reagan's first term.

choices.content: His approach to politics was relentlessly sunny and optimistic.
He believed that the United States would soon prosper thanks to the fall of the Soviet Union during his first term in office.
He thought that the United States should make a clean break with its past and embrace new social welfare programs modeled on those of Scandinavian nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
When **Mikhail Gorbachev** became the Premier of the Soviet Union in 1985, he pivoted to a policy of

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Gorbachev's approach marked a change from previous Soviet policy.

choices.content: openness and political restructuring.
relentless expansion of Soviet borders and political influence.
nuclear brinksmanship.

assessment_items.question_content:
Why was it an issue that the US government sold anti-tank weapons to Iran, and used the proceeds from that sale to fund anti-communist rebels in Nicaragua?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: In general, the United States supported anticommunist regimes.

choices.content: Congress had embargoed the sale of arms to Iran, and limited US assistance to the Nicaraguan Contras—both actions were illegal.
US Congress had expressly forbidden government support for anti-communist regimes worldwide.
Anti-tank weapons were outlawed by the Geneva Convention as weapons of mass destruction.

assessment_items.question_content:
How did gay rights activists draw attention to the **AIDS crisis**, which had been ignored by the Reagan administration, in 1987?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The Kent State Massacre occurred in 1970 and concerned Vietnam War protesters, not gay rights activists.

choices.content: They created an enormous quilt that covered Washington, D.C.’s entire National Mall, memorializing the many who had died from AIDS.
They conducted a speaking tour throughout Europe, putting political pressure on the United States from its allies in the United Nations.
They protested on the grounds of Kent State University, and four activists were killed by the Ohio National Guard in the famous Kent State Massacre.

assessment_items.question_content:
After ignoring the crisis for his first term, how did President Reagan respond to the demands of ACT UP and other **AIDS rights activists**?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Reagan did not sign an Executive Order interning HIV/AIDS positive individuals.

choices.content: He established the President’s Commission on the HIV Epidemic, and approved legislation to fund half a billion dollars’ worth of HIV/AIDS research and education.
He refused to sign any legislation allocating funding for HIV/AIDS research, citing moral concerns.
He issued an Executive Order requiring the immediate internment of any HIV/AIDS positive individuals.
